text: During laparoscopic ovarian cystectomy, the surgeon performs hydrodissection of the left ovary using a vasopressin and normal saline solution to separate the ovarian cyst from the ovarian cortex. A hemorrhagic cyst is visible on the left ovary.

The surgeon uses monopolar energy to incise the ovarian cortex following hydrodissection with vasopressin and saline. Graspers are then used to separate the ovarian cortex from the left ovarian hemorrhagic cyst, revealing encapsulated old blood within the cyst. The ovarian cyst is successfully detached from the ovary without complications.
The surgeon uses a bipolar instrument to achieve hemostasis on the ovarian cortex, aided by the vasopressin dilution.

The surgeon performs hydrodissection of the right ovary using a laparoscopic needle to inject a dilution of vasopressin mixed with normal saline between the ovarian cortex and the ovarian cyst. This technique aids in separating the cyst from the cortex and assists in hemostasis.

The surgeon incises the ovarian cortex to separate it from the ovarian cyst, using a vasopressin dilution to facilitate the dissection. The cortex is grasped, and the right ovarian cyst is carefully shielded out.
The surgeon addresses a right ovarian dermoid cyst, noting its ease of dissection from the ovarian cortex compared to other types of cysts.
The surgeon shells out the right ovarian dermoid cyst from the ovary, utilizing the vasopressin dilution to aid in hemostasis and facilitate the dissection process.
